question.title: Consecutive Logins
question.desc: Find users with 3 or more consecutive days of login activity from the given login history.
question.setup: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_logins; CREATE TABLE user_logins (user_id INT, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO user_logins (user_id, login_date) VALUES (1, '2022-01-01'), (1, '2022-01-02'), (1, '2022-01-03'), (2, '2022-01-05'), (2, '2022-01-06'), (3, '2022-01-01'), (3, '2022-01-02');
question.solution: WITH consecutive_logins AS ( SELECT user_id, login_date, DATEADD('day', -ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date), login_date) AS group_id FROM user_logins ) SELECT user_id, MIN(login_date) AS start_date, MAX(login_date) AS end_date FROM consecutive_logins GROUP BY user_id, group_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT login_date) >= 3;
question.tips: Consider using a calendar table for more complex date range queries.
hints: Use window functions to assign a group ID based on consecutive dates
Partition the data by user and apply the ROW_NUMBER function to get a sequence of dates
Utilize the DATEADD function to shift dates and group consecutive logins

tests.setup: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_logins; CREATE TABLE user_logins (user_id INT, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO user_logins (user_id, login_date) VALUES (4, '2022-01-10'), (4, '2022-01-11'), (4, '2022-01-12'), (5, '2022-01-15');

DROP TABLE IF EXISTS user_logins; CREATE TABLE user_logins (user_id INT, login_date DATE); INSERT INTO user_logins (user_id, login_date) VALUES (6, '2022-02-01'), (6, '2022-02-02'), (7, '2022-02-05'), (7, '2022-02-06'), (7, '2022-02-07');
